feedback.text: We need to ensure that purchased digital products remain functional even after official support ends. This means software, programs, games, apps etc. As long as the product is sold to the consumer as sale or purchase and not rental or lease, it should remain available to the customer as long as they want to and should have access to it. This has not been the case for many games especially. Great example of game not available to those who purchased the game is game called "The Crew" as its publisher Ubisoft closed the official servers and even physical copies of the game remain unplayable, even the single player portion of the game, which it did have. What Ubisoft should have done is allowed players of the game download some if not all of the map data, and then play it offline. What Ubisoft did instead was they removed it from players game libraries altogether, especially those copies they had sold on their own platform, now known as Ubisoft Connect (Formerly Uplay). And instead of refunding anything, they told customers to purchase one of the game sequels (Crew 2, Crew Motorfest). This remains a problem to consumer rights now on the digital age, and should have some clarity to ensure the practice of basic commerce; You pay for the product, and you can keep the product. Problem is, with digital products, publisher has the ability to make the product unusable to the consumer, especially if the product requires online connectivity. What should be done is force publishers of digital products to have End-Of-Life (EOL) plans to their products, and make them ensure the products they sold remain usable after official support ends. While there are security concerns if the product is able to connect for example, unofficial servers after official support has ended, that risk should remain on the consumer, should they remain using the product online this way. Also, the publisher is no longer liable for any harm following the use of product after official support has ended. As official support has ended, what should remain usable for the consumer is the so called Core product. In case of a Program, for example, Photo editor, user should remain able to use it for editing photos. If the program had online component, for example, stock photo server, users should be able to download images from the server before optional support ends, and should be given a notification of this becoming unusable, should the publisher end the support, but that is not a core feature of the program, and thus can be removed. Should publisher release some form of server functionality for the users they should not be able to monetize the product, and not should the Intellectual property be transferred, as those rights remain with the publisher. Should the publisher go bankrupt or close down, then products they sold may not remain available, but should another company purchase the intellectual property, and decide to sell the product again, previous owners should have access to the via their earlier purchase.
